narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 39—above the typical 36.7 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 44.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students' academic paths, family backgrounds, and bar outcomes, focusing on whether someone is likely to pass right away. The model's call hinged most on three key factors from your profile: your LSAT score, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 (well below the average 5.53), and your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 (lower than the typical 3.21). Your strong LSAT was the biggest positive force, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, as it signals sharp analytical skills that often correlate with success. In contrast, your lower Year 3 decile ranking had the next strongest pull toward failure, suggesting challenges in maintaining high performance later in your undergrad years compared to peers. Your undergraduate GPA reinforced that concern, nudging the odds further toward a first-try fail since it falls short of what's usually needed to build momentum for the bar. While your middle family income bracket and full-time student status align with most applicants, they didn't sway the balance much here. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having an edge in LSAT but lagging in sustained grades, which the model sees as the bigger risk for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible or bolstering your GPA through post-bac courses or a master's program to shift those key signals. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and explore schools with holistic reviews that might weigh your strengths more favorably. With focus on grade improvement, you can reshape this story and strengthen your next application.
